Re-reading Question & Options:
Which of the following best describes the working time of impression material?
A: After the start of appearing elastic properties...
B: Just after the start of appearing elastic properties...
C: From mix to just before elastic properties begin...
D: Loss of lusture...
All four options describe different phases of impression material handling.
Critical point: Standard dental principles state that:
→ Working time = duration from onset of elasticity until loss of plasticity/set
→ Elasticity appears ~mid-working time
→ Material becomes usable immediately upon gaining elasticity
→ So working time begins at or right after appearance of elasticity
Thus according to established dental science:
Working time starts when elastic properties appear → therefore A is correct (since it says "after", meaning starting point)
But note: some texts define working time as beginning exactly when elasticity appears.
Now compare:
A: "After" → implies later than onset → too late
B: "Just after" → very close to onset → plausible
C: Before elasticity → incorrect
D: Loss of lusture → that’s usually end-of-life/time of set → not working time
In dental textbooks like those by Ricketts or Dentsply:
“Working time” begins when material gains elasticity (i.e., when it becomes tacky/elastic).
So ideal definition: starts at onset of elasticity.
Among options:
- C is wrong (starts BEFORE elasticity?)
- D is wrong (loss of lusture = final stage)
- A (“after”) suggests delay — likely inaccurate
- B (“just after”) captures proximity to actual start
Best match in terminology: "just after" aligns with practical use where dentist works immediately post-elastification.
Moreover, many references say "impression material has begun working when it develops visible elasticity."
